 =20
   =20
      Good tax trade
   =20
 =20
The good tax trade is:=09You pay me $200.=09You deduct $200 from your taxab=
le income, saving you about $80 in taxes.=09I pay you $150.=09The $150 is n=
ot taxable income to you, so you pay no taxes on it.I make $50 from this tr=
ade (the $200 I get in Step 1 minus the $150 I pay in Step 3).[1] You make =
$30 (the $150 you get in Step=C2=A04, plus the $80 you save in Step 2, minu=
s the $200 you pay in Step 1). We can do this trade all day! Presumably the=
 Internal Revenue Service loses $80 on this trade.It is tricky to find trad=
es like this, because most things are taxable income, so Step 4 is a proble=
m.[2] Still, they exist. We actually  talked about one=C2=A0last week, one =
that is near to my heart because I did it as a job for years. Specifically =
we discussed a =E2=80=9Ccall options overlay=E2=80=9D transaction that Tesl=
a Inc. did in 2014 (and that ended with JPMorgan Chase & Co. suing it for $=
162 million last week). The basic economic logic of this transaction is tha=
t Tesla issued a convertible bond with a $359.87 conversion price, bought b=
ack the $359.87 conversion option from its banks (in a transaction called a=
 =E2=80=9Cbond hedge=E2=80=9D), and sold the banks an option to buy Tesla s=
tock at $560.64 (the =E2=80=9Cwarrant=E2=80=9D). As I wrote last week:The o=
verall result is that the company has effectively issued a synthetic high-p=
remium convertible: If the stock goes up 80% or 100%, it will issue some sh=
ares on the convertible but get them back from the bond hedge; it only =E2=
=80=9Creally=E2=80=9D issues shares if the stock goes up more than 122% and=
 the warrant is in the money.But the=C2=A0other, sometimes more important, =
reason that companies do this transaction is for taxes. When the company bu=
ys a bond hedge from its banks, it pays them money, which is tax-deductible=
; when it sells them a warrant, they pay it money, which is=C2=A0not taxabl=
e income.[3] Tesla paid about $603 million for its bond hedges in 2014 and =
got back about $389 million for its warrants. Assuming counterfactually tha=
t Tesla deducted that full $603 million from its income and paid a 35% tax =
rate,[4] the cash flows are:=09Tesla pays its banks $603 million for the bo=
nd hedge;=09It gets back $389 million from the banks for the warrants; and=
=09It gets back $211 million from the IRS=C2=A0from the tax deduction on th=
e bond hedge.Basically the call options overlay paid for itself: Tesla paid=
 $603 million and got back about $600 million. But you could adjust the tra=
nsaction a bit =E2=80=94 for instance=C2=A0by selling the banks more valuab=
le warrants (with a lower exercise price) =E2=80=94=C2=A0and=C2=A0have it g=
enerate money.This is a nice version of the good tax trade that paid my sal=
ary at an investment bank for a while, and that is explicitly blessed by th=
is 2007 IRS guidance. The trick is that, for a corporation, transactions in=
 your own equity (like the warrant) do not generate taxable income, but bon=
d transactions =E2=80=94=C2=A0including both the convertible bond and the b=
ond hedge that =E2=80=9Chedges=E2=80=9D the conversion option in the bond =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0do. So your outflows are deductible bond-related payments, w=
hile your inflows are non-taxable equity payments.There are other versions =
of the good tax trade that are not so clearly allowed. A classic fun  area=
=C2=A0of inquiry is credit-card rewards. Credit-card reward points are=C2=
=A0kind of=C2=A0like your credit-card company paying you cash, but they are=
 also=C2=A0kind of=C2=A0like just getting a discount on your purchases; for=
 many purposes they are not quite taxable income. If you are a business and=
 you could just pay your credit-card issuer $110 for $100 of reward points,=
 then maybe you could deduct the $110 payment=C2=A0as a business expense, b=
ut then not count the $100 of reward points as taxable income?=C2=A0Worth a=
 shot? Here=E2=80=99s a fun Wall Street Journal article about some American=
 Express Co. salespeople who gave it a go:Salespeople focused on business c=
ustomers whose vendors didn=E2=80=99t accept AmEx cards. Businesses that us=
ed AmEx=E2=80=99s wire services to pay vendors would have to pay fees of be=
tween about 1.77% and 3.5%, according to AmEx training and sales documents =
and people familiar with the matter.Business owners were told that they cou=
ld deduct those fees from their businesses=E2=80=99 taxable income as ordin=
ary expenses, the people said. The strategy had another benefit, business o=
wners were told: They could earn reward points and could convert them into =
untaxed cash using the AmEx Platinum Charles Schwab card, the people said.H=
ere=E2=80=99s how the tax math was described in an AmEx document viewed by =
the Journal: A business owner would use AmEx=E2=80=99s wire services to sen=
d $10 million for a 1.77% fee=E2=80=94or $177,000. Assuming the business ow=
ner would pay a 42% combined federal and state marginal income-tax rate, th=
e owner would deduct the fee for a $74,340 reduction in taxes, lowering the=
 transaction=E2=80=99s net cost to $102,660.The business owner would also e=
arn one point per dollar spent, or 10 million points. The owner could then =
transfer the points to a personal AmEx Platinum Charles Schwab card at 1.25=
 cents per point, generating a cash reward of $125,000. Subtract the net tr=
ansaction cost of $102,660 for a gain of $22,340.The rewards are 1.25% of y=
our spending, the fees are 1.77% of your spending, at a 42% tax rate the ne=
t fees are 1.03% of your spending,=C2=A0so you make 0.22% =E2=80=94=C2=A0as=
suming that the fees are tax-deductible but the rewards are not taxable. Th=
ese were not great assumptions! But who was going to check? Not the IRS, pr=
obably, assumed the AmEx salespeople; there=E2=80=99s a long tradition of l=
ooking the other way with credit-card rewards. Not AmEx=E2=80=99s or the cl=
ient=E2=80=99s lawyers or accountants, assumed the salespeople, because=C2=
=A0no one would tell them:=E2=80=9CNo staff, CPAs or Tax Attorney=E2=80=99s=
,=E2=80=9D a sales director wrote in an email to salespeople who were tryin=
g to sign up a prospective client.Brandon Pope last year fielded the tax pi=
tch on behalf of a wealthy entrepreneur whose finances he manages. A skepti=
cal Mr. Pope said he asked to speak with an AmEx lawyer. His request, he sa=
id, was declined.=E2=80=9CNo AmEx attorney nor IRS attorney will take a har=
d line on this since it=E2=80=99s such a grey area and always has been,=E2=
=80=9D the AmEx salesperson wrote in an email to Mr. Pope.Mr. Pope said tha=
t he and his client stopped talking to AmEx.Also!Sales teams were told to d=
iscontinue the tax pitch during an early 2020 company call, according to pe=
ople familiar with the matter. Vice presidents and directors on the sales t=
eams later told salespeople they could continue making the pitch as long as=
 they didn=E2=80=99t leave documentation with customers, according to peopl=
e familiar with the matter.This is not legal advice but here=E2=80=99s a qu=
ick taxonomy of products that you should or should not pitch:=09Products th=
at your company tells you to pitch: Probably pitch these.=09Products that y=
our company tells you not to pitch: Probably do not pitch these.=09Products=
 that your company officially tells you not to pitch but that your boss uno=
fficially tells you to pitch anyway: Definitely do not pitch these.=09Produ=
cts that your company tells you not to pitch but that your boss tells you t=
o pitch anyway as long as you=E2=80=99re sure not to put it in writing: Cal=

rnal Revenue Service alleging that AmEx knowingly persuaded business owners=
 to underreport their income and taxes.=E2=80=9D The Justice Department and=

      Elon Markets Hypothesis
   =20
 =20
The basic promise of financial markets is that if enough people want someth=
ing badly enough, the market will provide it, though it may turn out not to=
 be exactly what they want. If people are clamoring for safe assets, the ma=
rket will dutifully make a ton of risky mortgages and tranche them into saf=
e assets, which will eventually blow up. If people are clamoring for electr=
ic-vehicle investments, a bunch of pre-revenue electric-vehicle companies w=
ill go public at huge valuations. And if the thing that people want in life=
 more than anything else is Tesla Inc. volatility, well, Tesla will be vola=
tile:One of Tesla=E2=80=99s oddest quirks is the fuel that has helped power=
 its rocketing stock market value. Although its stock is wildly popular wit=
h many ordinary retail investors, the swelling size and hyperactivity of Te=
sla =E2=80=9Coptions=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94 popular derivatives contracts that =
allow investors to bet both on and against a stock and magnify any gains an=
d losses =E2=80=94 has also flabbergasted many market veterans.The nominal =
trading value of Tesla options has averaged $241bn a day in recent weeks, a=
ccording to Goldman Sachs. That compares with $138bn a day for Amazon, the =
second most active single-stock option market, and $112bn a day for the res=
t of the S&P 500 index combined. This makes Tesla=E2=80=99s stock more pron=
e to whipsaw movements, because of the =E2=80=9Cleverage=E2=80=9D inherent =
in using options to trade. =E2=80=A6The value of options depend on what the=
 underlying shares do, but due to their complex mechanics analysts say the =
option tail can occasionally wag the equity dog if there is enough activity=
 in them, and even bleed into the broader stock market =E2=80=94 adding to =
its churn and making it harder to navigate for many investors.Curnutt point=
s out that it is unprecedented to have such a huge stock that is also so vo=
latile, and moves to the beat of its own drum.=C2=A0The basic deal with opt=
ions is that when you buy an option from a dealer, the dealer will hedge th=
e option by buying or selling the underlying stock;=C2=A0in particular the =
dealer will adjust its hedge by buying the stock when it goes up and sellin=
g it when it goes down. This makes the stock more volatile: When it goes up=
, options dealers are buying and pushing it up more; when it goes down, the=
y=E2=80=99re selling=C2=A0and pushing it down more. Dealers who sell option=
s are said to be =E2=80=9Cselling volatility.=E2=80=9D They produce volatil=
ity with their trading and sell it to customers. Customers want a lot of Te=
sla volatility. So a lot of Tesla volatility is produced and delivered to t=
hem. The market gives people what they want.The block quote is from a story=
 from Robin Wigglesworth in the Financial Times about some of the weird stu=
ff floating around Tesla:The real importance and wider footprint of what mi=
ght be called the =E2=80=9CTesla-financial complex=E2=80=9D far outstrips t=
he company=E2=80=99s market capitalisation. This is thanks to a vast, tangl=
ed web of dependent investment vehicles, corporate emulators and an enormou=
s associated derivatives market of unparalleled breadth, depth and hyperact=
ivity.Combined, these factors mean Tesla=E2=80=99s influence over the ebb a=
nd flow of the stock market is far greater than even its size would imply. =
It may even be historically unrivalled in its wider impact, some analysts s=
ay.=E2=80=9CWe don=E2=80=99t really have the language to describe Tesla any=
 more,=E2=80=9D says Michael Green, chief strategist at Simplify Asset Mana=
gement. =E2=80=9CIt=E2=80=99s like explaining to a person in a two-dimensio=
nal world the concept of =E2=80=98up=E2=80=99.=E2=80=9DAnd:=E2=80=9CIt=E2=
=80=99s the original meme stock,=E2=80=9D says Green, referring to companie=
s like GameStop that have gained sky-high valuations off the back of social=
 media hype. =E2=80=9CShorting Tesla is just an ego trade at this stage. Te=
sla has been a primary contributor to destroying the credibility of active =
management over the past few years.=E2=80=9DHe doesn=E2=80=99t even mention=
 Dogecoin! Around here I talk sometimes about the =E2=80=9C Elon Markets Hy=
pothesis,=E2=80=9D the idea that =E2=80=9Cthe way finance works now is that=
 things are valuable not based on their cash flows but on their proximity t=
o Elon Musk.=E2=80=9D Musk=E2=80=99s public statements about Tesla=E2=80=99=
s stock can move the stock, but his public statement about Bitcoin can move=
 Bitcoin, and his public statements about other cryptocurrencies can=C2=A0r=
eally=C2=A0 move those cryptocurrencies, and his public statements about me=
ssaging apps can move the stocks of  totally unrelated companies with simil=
ar names to those messaging apps, and Hertz Global Holdings Inc.  now trade=
s on Tesla hype, and there is a  vibrant secondary market in Tesla-branded =
satin short shorts, and=C2=A0on and on.If you want weird financial instrume=
nts that are obscurely correlated to public perceptions of Tesla, Tesla cal=
l options are beginner stuff. Short the short shorts! Do DOGE/SHIB relative=
-value trades! Buy stock in some unrelated retailer and put out a press rel=
ease asking it to  open charging stations for Teslas!=C2=A0Basically the po=
int is that there is an entire global financial system and you can just arb=
itrarily pick any part of it and say =E2=80=9Cwhat does Tesla mean for this=
?=E2=80=9D and generate some plausible answer that could inform your tradin=
g.=C2=A0The Tesla-financial complex is the financial complex.Elsewhere here=
 is Christopher Kardatzke on =E2=80=9CBuilding the =E2=80=98Musk Proximity =
Metric=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D:While there are a number of qualitative ways to at=
tempt this, I tried to implement a quantitative method by examining the deg=
rees of separation between different companies=E2=80=99 Twitter accounts an=
d @elonmusk.I=E2=80=99m not convinced that that is the right way to measure=
 Musk proximity,=C2=A0but in any case soon there will be graduate-level qua=
ntitative finance classes about this question. This is what finance is now.

I  wrote last month that =E2=80=9Cone good rule of thumb is that if you are=
 on an internet message board looking to hire a hit man, and you end up cha=
tting with an enthusiastic poster who seems like he might do a good job of =
murdering the person you want murdered, the odds that he is in fact an unde=
rcover police officer are nearly 100%.=E2=80=9D This was too late for Wendy=
 Wein!Wein wanted her ex-husband dead. But she didn=E2=80=99t want to kill =
him herself and didn=E2=80=99t know anyone she trusted to do it for her. So=
 she did what a lot of people do when they have a job they can=E2=80=99t or=
 don=E2=80=99t want to do themselves =E2=80=94 she searched for help on the=
 Internet.What Wein found was presumably reassuring. The website promised h=
er confidentiality. It boasted of industry awards. It showed off testimonia=
ls of satisfied customers, including one from Laura S., who had =E2=80=9Cca=
ught my husband cheating with the babysitter.=E2=80=9D The website bragged =
about complying with HIPPA, which it said was =E2=80=9Cthe Hitman Informati=
on Privacy & Protection Act of 1964,=E2=80=9D a nod to the Health Insurance=
 Portability and Accountability Act, or HIPAA, the law passed in 1996 to pr=
otect patients=E2=80=99 medical information.The trouble for Wein was that R=
entAHitman.com is a fake website. It=E2=80=99s not run by =E2=80=9CGuido Fa=
nelli,=E2=80=9D as it claims, but by Bob Innes, a 54-year-old Northern Cali=
fornia man who forwards any serious inquiries to law enforcement. Innes lau=
nched the site 16 years ago as part of an Internet security business that n=
ever went anywhere. Instead, it has served as a honeypot of sorts, attracti=
ng people who want to hire professional killers.For Wein, it didn=E2=80=99t=
 go well. She was arrested within days of seeking out an assassin and plead=
ed guilty earlier this month to solicitation of murder and using a computer=
 to commit a crime.=C2=A0=E2=80=A6All these years later, he=E2=80=99s still=
 a little dumbfounded people don=E2=80=99t realize his site is bogus.=E2=80=
=9CI don=E2=80=99t get it,=E2=80=9D Innes told The Washington Post last wee=
k. =E2=80=9CPeople are just stupid.=E2=80=9DI don=E2=80=99t get it either.=
=C2=A0We have  talked about RentAHitman.com before. I joked, =E2=80=9Cbe su=
re to visit my new website, dosomeinsidertrading.com, where you can email m=
e misappropriated material nonpublic information and I can=C2=A0either=C2=
=A0insider trade on it=C2=A0or=C2=A0alert the authorities, I can=E2=80=99t =
wait for you to find out which it is.=E2=80=9D Since then someone really di=
d register that domain. Go ahead and click!=C2=A0I suppose here I should ge=
sture at=C2=A0a joke about Web3, immutable transparent blockchain-based cre=
dentials, etc.? Here is Adam Davidson on the dream of Web3=C2=A0for hiring:=
The conceit of web3 is that an online identity that can not be faked or alt=
ered offers a new level of trust and transparency. (This word, transparency=
, may not be the right one. On the one hand, your blockchain identity is fu=
lly transparent. Anyone can see every single thing ever done with that iden=
tity. On the other hand, it is trivially easy to prevent anyone from knowin=
g who, in the real world, that identity belongs to.) =E2=80=A6With web3, a =
candidate can share a far more robust career story than whatever is shown o=
n their LinkedIn profile. They can show not just what titles they had, but =
what projects they worked on, what courses they took, what communities they=
 contributed to.Of course, someone could tell you all that now. But resumes=
 are, typically, works of creative aspirational fiction. With web3, people =
can share tokens showing someone=E2=80=99s crucial role on a project. This =
happens, now, on DAOs (more on those here), blockchain-based communities. I=
 spend a lot of time at the Bankless DAO, where it is just a routine part o=
f the daily flow for people to send tokens of gratitude to those who have b=
een helpful in some project or other. People who attend meetings, get a POA=
P, a token of attendance. So, you can see at a glance if someone is an acti=
ve member or an occasional lurker. Every day, people send =E2=80=9Ctips=E2=
=80=9D of tokens of gratitude to others. One person giving a particularly h=
elpful colleague a tip often leads to an avalanche of others doing the same=
. (Come to think of it, I really haven=E2=80=99t gotten a lot of tips. I sh=
ould up my game.)As web3 becomes more ubiquitous (as I expect it to), young=
er employees will ask their bosses and co-workers and, eventually, demand o=
f them that they implement this kind of in-real-time, collaborative credent=
ialing.Similarly, people can build up a collection of immutable tokens show=
ing the conferences they attended, the courses they took, the skills they p=
roved, the books they read, the creators they supported.I expect it to beco=
me routine--first among younger people in more technical fields--that recru=
itment will involve looking at someone=E2=80=99s blockchain-backed identity=
 to see a richer picture of the person=E2=80=99s background. If a candidate=
 with an unpromising resume can demonstrate an unusually large number of th=
ese Gold Star tokens--in which former bosses and colleagues sent digital ex=
pressions of gratitude--from their past, you might overlook the fact that t=
hey didn=E2=80=99t, quite, graduate college. Alternatively, if someone with=
 pristine credentials has a dearth of such expressions of gratitude, you mi=
ght look at them more critically.And in particular here he is on the dream =
of Web3 for=C2=A0ad hoc, project-based hiring:If you want to achieve some q=
uick result--design an app, create a conference, build a new kind of bicycl=
e, whatever--that you couldn=E2=80=99t do on your own, you typically have t=
o spend a ton of time and resources NOT doing the thing you care about. You=
 probably need to hire people, which means you need to raise money, which m=
eans you need a business plan and have to spend most of your time pitching.=
 =E2=80=A6With web3, you could announce your goal publicly. People who are =
interested will reach out. You can quickly see what they=E2=80=99ve done (s=
ee above) and choose the team that meets your requirements. You can create =
a coin or token and distribute it among the team members. You can have a sy=
stem that grants more of these coins when someone is recognized by the rest=
 of the team for a helpful contribution. Ownership, then, will be dynamic a=
nd will reflect real activity.When the thing you all are building launches,=
 the profits can be distributed through the coin system, and the people who=
 added the most value will get the most reward.Isn=E2=80=99t that great? I =
mean, in general the potential=C2=A0future he describes is both intriguing =
and also horrifying, but here I am just talking about hit men. If you want =
to achieve some quick result =E2=80=94=C2=A0murder your husband =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0you can announce your goal publicly and people who are interested wil=
l reach out. You can quickly see what they=E2=80=99ve done and choose the h=
it man who meets your requirements. A hit man who has received a lot of Goo=
d Job Killing My Husband tokens from satisfied customers will be an attract=
ive candidate, and you might pay a premium for his services. A hit man who =
has received a lot of FBI Gold Star tokens from the Federal Bureau of Inves=
tigation would be a very=C2=A0bad=C2=A0candidate and you should=C2=A0not=C2=
=A0hire him despite his no doubt very reasonable rates. Immutable transpare=
nt digital identity! The fact that you have posted your goal publicly proba=
bly isn=E2=80=99t great for your odds of not getting arrested, I don=E2=80=
=99t know.

      Elsewhere in Web3
   =20
 =20
The classic Ronald Coase model of the firm is that firms exist to optimize =
transaction costs. Some transactions occur in the market using the price me=
chanism, but it would be a pain to hire a new group of freelancers and nego=
tiate their pay every time you want to do a new project, and so in practice=
 companies exist with permanent salaried employees who can be told to do ne=
w projects without going through new market transactions. One promise of th=
e internet was that it would reduce transaction costs: Technology makes it =
easier for buyers and sellers of goods and services to coordinate with each=
 other, so they don=E2=80=99t need the coordinating abilities of the firm. =
In recent years this has led to a surge of interest in non-firm ways of coo=
rdinating behavior, from gig-economy platforms (lots of people drive for Ub=
er, but are not=C2=A0employees of Uber) to the Web3 projects that Davidson =
writes about to decentralized autonomous organizations in which people pool=
 together to make joint decisions without a corporate management structure.=
On the other hand, these days those projects tend to be built on crypto, wh=
ich introduces huge new artificial transaction costs to mess it all up. Her=
e is a Motherboard article about the people who donated $40 million to Cons=
titutionDAO, failed to buy the Constitution, and now can=E2=80=99t get thei=
r money back because it will all be eaten up by transaction fees:In its "ho=
w to donate" video, a member of ConstitutionDAO recommended that donors add=
 "recommend adding about $150 to $200 more than you'd like to contribute" t=
o their donations to pay gas fees, which are transaction fees on the Ethere=
um network. Motherboard contributed a small amount of money to the project =
to see how this would play out in practice. Here is how it worked:Constitut=
ionDAO accepted only ether, the token on Ethereum. For someone to convert U=
SD to $PEOPLE tokens, the process had several steps. First, we had to buy E=
thereum on an exchange (we used Coinbase). We bought $200 worth of Ethereum=
. Coinbase took a $3 fee. Then, we had to send the Ethereum from Coinbase t=
o a MetaMask crypto wallet. To do this, we had to pay a $12 network fee. Th=
en, we had to send the Ethereum from MetaMask to Juicebox. So-called "gas" =
fees vary wildly and depend on how busy the Ethereum network is at any give=
n moment and the complexity of the transaction. Right now, gas fees on Ethe=
reum are very high, and a highly complex operation could end up costing hun=
dreds of dollars in fees. In our case, we paid a $75 gas fee to contribute =
roughly $75 to the project. Of the initial $200 we bought in ETH, $90 was e=
aten up in fees simply to donate to ConstitutionDAO. =E2=80=A6In order to g=
et a refund, we have to do this in reverse, basically. And so to get our ET=
H =C2=A0back from Juicebox, we would have to pay gas fees again, meaning es=
sentially the entirety of the amount invested would be wiped out.=C2=A0=E2=
=80=A6Interactions with the Juicebox contract on the Ethereum blockchain re=
veal numerous instances of people getting their money back only to have it =
significantly reduced by fees or wiped out entirely. Here's someone transfe=
rring .011 ETH ($46) out and paying .015 ETH ($63) in fees, meaning they en=
ded up paying $18 for their $0 refund. Here's someone else getting .018 ETH=
 ($76) refunded and paying .0175 ETH ($74) in fees, so they got $2 back whe=
n all was said and done.=C2=A0Honestly this does not seem like progress?

 Eurozone banks told to do more to tackle climate change risks. China Slows=

er Assets to Back Consumers=E2=80=99 Policies. Binance in talks with sovere=
ign wealth funds as it seeks investments. Is the Four-Day Workweek Finally =
Within Our Grasp? World Cup Host Qatar Used Ex-CIA Officer to  Spy on FIFA.=
 Goldman Analyst Turned  Bake Off Star Finds City of London Fame. Lord of t=
he Rings Crypto Bid Fails in  Tolkien Brand Pushback. =E2=80=9CAfter a four=
-hour drive back to West Point, they unveiled not Bill No. 37 but Bill No. =
35, an arthritic, 14-year-old retiree with only one horn.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0If =
you'd like to get=C2=A0Money=C2=A0Stuff=C2=A0in handy email form, right in =
your inbox, please=C2=A0subscribe at this link. Or you can subscribe to Mon=
ey Stuff and other great Bloomberg newsletters here. Thanks![1] What about =
*my*=C2=A0taxes? They are outside the scope of the problem. Probably I am a=
 bank and my $50 of net income from the transaction is taxable to me. Perha=
ps I am some sort of exempt entity and it is not. The important point is th=
at in general it will *not* be the case that the money I get in Step 1 is t=
axable while the money I pay in Step 3 is not deductible; there is no tight=
 connection between =E2=80=9Ctaxable to me=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Cdeductible=
 for you=E2=80=9D or vice versa. If the result of this trade is that I pay =
$80 of taxes on my $200 of income from Step 1, and have no deduction on my =
$150 expense in Step 3, then this trade makes no sense for me and I won=E2=
=80=99t do it. But that=E2=80=99s not generally how it works. (Obviously th=
e ideal trade is that the money I pay in Step 3 is deductible while the mon=
ey I get in Step 1 is *not* taxable to me, that is, *each* of us gets a ded=
uction but no income. That seems very hard to achieve! And honestly like fl=
ying too close to the sun! I know of no trades like that, but I feel like I=
=E2=80=99m going to get at least five of them emailed to me.)[2] For indivi=
dual taxpayers, Step 2 can also be a problem, because  most expenses for in=
dividuals are not tax-deductible. For businesses, most expenses are deducti=
ble, which makes this easier.[3] The actual mechanism is a bit more complic=
ated =E2=80=94=C2=A0you deduct the cost of the bond hedge as original issue=
 discount over the life of the convertible bond =E2=80=94=C2=A0but that is =
schematically accurate.[4] Counterfactually because, among other things, yo=
u deduct the bond hedge premium over time=C2=A0and because Tesla had a net =
loss in 2014 so did not have much use for a $603 million tax deduction. Des=
pite how nicely the numbers match up here, I assume that Tesla was not prim=
arily concerned with tax savings when it did this trade; it lost money ever=
y year from 2014 through 2019 so was not paying too much in taxes. Other, m=
ore tax-paying companies sometimes do this more for tax reasons though. Not=
e that the U.S. federal corporate tax rate is now 21%; it was 35% in 2014.

10px;">
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><strong>Programming note:</strong><em> Money S=
tuff will be off for the rest of the week. Have a happy Thanksgiving, and I=
=E2=80=99ll see you back here on Monday.</em></p><table class=3D"header" wi=
dth=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" cellpadding=3D"0">
<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=
-table-lspace: 0pt; mso-table-rspace: 0pt;">
<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Good tax trade</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The good tax trade is:</p><ol style=3D=
"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=09<li styl=
e=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">You pay me $200.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-botto=
m: 5px;">You deduct $200 from your taxable income, saving you about $80 in =
taxes.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">I pay you $150.</li>=09<li =
style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The $150 is not taxable income to you, so you=
 pay no taxes on it.</li></ol><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I make $50 from =
this trade (the $200 I get in Step 1 minus the $150 I pay in Step 3).[1] Yo=
u make $30 (the $150 you get in Step=C2=A04, plus the $80 you save in Step =
2, minus the $200 you pay in Step 1). We can do this trade all day! Presuma=
bly the Internal Revenue Service loses $80 on this trade.</p><p style=3D"ma=
rgin: 16px 0;">It is tricky to find trades like this, because most things a=
re taxable income, so Step 4 is a problem.[2] Still, they exist. We actuall=
y <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/25806429.278654/=
aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMTEtMTYvanB=
tb3JnYW4tZmlnaHRzLXRlc2xhLW92ZXItd2FycmFudHM_Y21waWQ9QkJEMTEyMzIxX01PTkVZU1=
RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjExM=
TIzJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB40cb1dfd" item=
prop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color=
: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bord=
er-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #cc=
c;"> talked about one=C2=A0last week</a>, one that is near to my heart beca=
use I did it as a job for years. Specifically we discussed a =E2=80=9Ccall =
options overlay=E2=80=9D transaction that Tesla Inc. did in 2014 (and that =
ended with JPMorgan Chase & Co. suing it for $162 million last week). T=
he basic economic logic of this transaction is that Tesla issued a converti=
ble bond with a $359.87 conversion price, bought back the $359.87 conversio=
n option from its banks (in a transaction called a =E2=80=9Cbond hedge=E2=
=80=9D), and sold the banks an option to buy Tesla stock at $560.64 (the =
=E2=80=9Cwarrant=E2=80=9D). As I wrote last week:</p><blockquote><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The overall result is that the company has effectively=
 issued a synthetic high-premium convertible: If the stock goes up 80% or 1=
00%, it will issue some shares on the convertible but get them back from th=
e bond hedge; it only =E2=80=9Creally=E2=80=9D issues shares if the stock g=
oes up more than 122% and the warrant is in the money.</p></blockquote><p s=
tyle=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But the=C2=A0<em>other</em>, sometimes <em>more im=
portant</em>, reason that companies do this transaction is for taxes. When =
the company buys a bond hedge from its banks, it pays them money, which is =
tax-deductible; when it sells them a warrant, they pay it money, which is=
=C2=A0<em>not taxable income</em>.[3] Tesla <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bl=
oombergbusiness.com/click/25806429.278654/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoa=
XZlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzEzMTg2MDUvMDAwMTU2NDU5MDE1MDAxMDMxL3RzbGEtMTBrXzIwMTQx=
MjMxLmh0bQ/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB20db7074" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemsc=
ope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" title=3D"see page 57" style=3D"color: #=
000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-=
bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"=
>paid about $603 million</a> for its bond hedges in 2014 and got back about=
 $389 million for its warrants. Assuming counterfactually that Tesla deduct=
ed that full $603 million from its income and paid a 35% tax rate,[4] the c=
ash flows are:</p><ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px=
; margin: 16px 0;">=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Tesla pays its bank=
s $603 million for the bond hedge;</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;"=
>It gets back $389 million from the banks for the warrants; and</li>=09<li =
style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">It gets back $211 million from the IRS=C2=A0f=
rom the tax deduction on the bond hedge.</li></ol><p style=3D"margin: 16px =
0;">Basically the call options overlay paid for itself: Tesla paid $603 mil=
lion and got back about $600 million. But you could adjust the transaction =
a bit =E2=80=94 for instance=C2=A0by selling the banks more valuable warran=
ts (with a lower exercise price) =E2=80=94=C2=A0and=C2=A0have it generate m=
oney.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This is a nice version of the good ta=
x trade that paid my salary at an investment bank for a while, and that is =
explicitly blessed by <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/cl=
ick/25806429.278654/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuaXJzLmdvdi9wdWIvaXJzLXV0bC9hbTIwMDcwMTQ=
ucGRm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB359f24af" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">this 2007 IRS guidance</=
a>. The trick is that, for a corporation, transactions in your own equity (=
like the warrant) do not generate taxable income, but bond transactions =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0including both the convertible bond and the bond hedge that =E2=
=80=9Chedges=E2=80=9D the conversion option in the bond =E2=80=94=C2=A0do. =
So your outflows are deductible bond-related payments, while your inflows a=
re non-taxable equity payments.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There are o=
ther versions of the good tax trade that are not so clearly allowed. A clas=
sic fun <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/25806429.2=
78654/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMDMtM=
Dkvc2VjLWdvZXMtYWZ0ZXItYW5hbHlzdC1mb3Jlc2hhZG93aW5nP2NtcGlkPUJCRDExMjMyMV9N=
T05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJ=
tPTIxMTEyMyZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB765263=
bd" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;"> area=C2=A0of inquiry</a> is credit-card rewards. Credit-card =
reward points are=C2=A0<em>kind of=C2=A0</em>like your credit-card company =
paying you cash, but they are also=C2=A0<em>kind of=C2=A0</em>like just get=
ting a discount on your purchases; for many purposes they are not quite tax=
able income. If you are a business and you could just pay your credit-card =
issuer $110 for $100 of reward points, then maybe you could deduct the $110=
 payment=C2=A0as a business expense, but then not count the $100 of reward =
points as taxable income?=C2=A0Worth a shot? Here=E2=80=99s <a href=3D"http=
s://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/25806429.278654/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud=
3NqLmNvbS9hcnRpY2xlcy9hbWV4LXBpdGNoZWQtYnVzaW5lc3MtY3VzdG9tZXJzLWEtdGF4LWJy=
ZWFrLXRoYXQtZG9lc250LWFkZC11cC0xMTYzNzU4Nzk4MD9tb2Q9aHBfbGlzdGFfcG9zNA/607f=
07ceef4b8524a319a23cB92a29429" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscop=
e" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !import=
ant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-=
style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">a fun Wall Street Journal article</a=
> about some American Express Co. salespeople who gave it a go:</p><blockqu=
ote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Salespeople focused on business customers =
whose vendors didn=E2=80=99t accept AmEx cards. Businesses that used AmEx=
=E2=80=99s wire services to pay vendors would have to pay fees of between a=
bout 1.77% and 3.5%, according to AmEx training and sales documents and peo=
ple familiar with the matter.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Business owne=
rs were told that they could deduct those fees from their businesses=E2=80=
=99 taxable income as ordinary expenses, the people said. The strategy had =
another benefit, business owners were told: They could earn reward points a=
nd could convert them into untaxed cash using the AmEx Platinum Charles Sch=
wab card, the people said.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here=E2=80=99s h=
ow the tax math was described in an AmEx document viewed by the Journal: A =
business owner would use AmEx=E2=80=99s wire services to send $10 million f=
or a 1.77% fee=E2=80=94or $177,000. Assuming the business owner would pay a=
 42% combined federal and state marginal income-tax rate, the owner would d=
educt the fee for a $74,340 reduction in taxes, lowering the transaction=E2=
=80=99s net cost to $102,660.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The business =
owner would also earn one point per dollar spent, or 10 million points. The=
 owner could then transfer the points to a personal AmEx Platinum Charles S=
chwab card at 1.25 cents per point, generating a cash reward of $125,000. S=
ubtract the net transaction cost of $102,660 for a gain of $22,340.</p></bl=
ockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The rewards are 1.25% of your spendin=
g, the fees are 1.77% of your spending, at a 42% tax rate the net fees are =
1.03% of your spending,=C2=A0so you make 0.22% =E2=80=94=C2=A0assuming that=
 the fees are tax-deductible but the rewards are not taxable. These were no=
t great assumptions! But who was going to check? Not the IRS, probably, ass=
umed the AmEx salespeople; there=E2=80=99s a long tradition of looking the =
other way with credit-card rewards. Not AmEx=E2=80=99s or the client=E2=80=
=99s lawyers or accountants, assumed the salespeople, because=C2=A0<em>no o=
ne would tell them:</em></p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=
=9CNo staff, CPAs or Tax Attorney=E2=80=99s,=E2=80=9D a sales director wrot=
e in an email to salespeople who were trying to sign up a prospective clien=
t.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Brandon Pope last year fielded the tax p=
itch on behalf of a wealthy entrepreneur whose finances he manages. A skept=
ical Mr. Pope said he asked to speak with an AmEx lawyer. His request, he s=
aid, was declined.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CNo AmEx attorne=
y nor IRS attorney will take a hard line on this since it=E2=80=99s such a =
grey area and always has been,=E2=80=9D the AmEx salesperson wrote in an em=
ail to Mr. Pope.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Mr. Pope said that he and =
his client stopped talking to AmEx.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16p=
x 0;">Also!</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Sales teams were to=
ld to discontinue the tax pitch during an early 2020 company call, accordin=
g to people familiar with the matter. Vice presidents and directors on the =
sales teams later told salespeople they could continue making the pitch as =
long as they didn=E2=80=99t leave documentation with customers, according t=
o people familiar with the matter.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px=
 0;">This is not legal advice but here=E2=80=99s a quick taxonomy of produc=
ts that you should or should not pitch:</p><ol style=3D"list-style-type: de=
cimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: =
5px;"><em>Products that your company tells you to pitch: </em>Probably pitc=
h these.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;"><em>Products that your co=
mpany tells you not to pitch:</em> Probably do not pitch these.</li>=09<li =
style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;"><em>Products that your company officially tel=
ls you not to pitch but that your boss unofficially tells you to pitch anyw=
ay: </em>Definitely do not pitch these.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: =
5px;"><em>Products that your company tells you not to pitch but that your b=
oss tells you to pitch anyway as long as you=E2=80=99re sure not to put it =
in writing:</em> Call the police!</li></ol><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=
=80=9CIn July, a whistleblower filed a report with the Internal Revenue Ser=
vice alleging that AmEx knowingly persuaded business owners to underreport =
their income and taxes.=E2=80=9D The Justice Department and various regulat=
ors are investigating, AmEx is doing an internal investigation, Charles Sch=
wab is horrified, it has all ended sordidly. But it was the good tax trade =
so you can see why they tried it.</p><table class=3D"liveintent-sponsored-c=
ontent" width=3D"auto" cellpadding=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0">

</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The basic promise of financial markets=
 is that if enough people want something badly enough, the market will prov=
ide it, though it may turn out not to be exactly what they want. If people =
are clamoring for safe assets, the market will dutifully make a ton of risk=
y mortgages and tranche them into safe assets, which will eventually blow u=
p. If people are clamoring for electric-vehicle investments, a bunch of pre=
-revenue electric-vehicle companies will go public at huge valuations. And =
if the thing that people want in life more than anything else is Tesla Inc.=
 volatility, well, <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click=
/25806429.278654/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZnQuY29tL2NvbnRlbnQvMTdmMGNkMWYtZTc1MS00ZG=
RiLWIxM2MtZWE0ZTY4NWI1NWMw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB7f97c361" itemprop=3D"S=
toryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000=
; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom=
-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Tesla=
 will be volatile</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One of T=
esla=E2=80=99s oddest quirks is the fuel that has helped power its rocketin=
g stock market value. Although its stock is wildly popular with many ordina=
ry retail investors, the swelling size and hyperactivity of Tesla =E2=80=9C=
options=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94 popular derivatives contracts that allow investo=
rs to bet both on and against a stock and magnify any gains and losses =E2=
=80=94 has also flabbergasted many market veterans.</p><p style=3D"margin: =
16px 0;">The nominal trading value of Tesla options has averaged $241bn a d=
ay in recent weeks, according to Goldman Sachs. That compares with $138bn a=
 day for Amazon, the second most active single-stock option market, and $11=
2bn a day for the rest of the S&P 500 index combined. This makes Tesla=
=E2=80=99s stock more prone to whipsaw movements, because of the =E2=80=9Cl=
everage=E2=80=9D inherent in using options to trade. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The value of options depend on what the underlying sha=
res do, but due to their complex mechanics analysts say the option tail can=
 occasionally wag the equity dog if there is enough activity in them, and e=
ven bleed into the broader stock market =E2=80=94 adding to its churn and m=
aking it harder to navigate for many investors.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px=
 0;">Curnutt points out that it is unprecedented to have such a huge stock =
that is also so volatile, and moves to the beat of its own drum.=C2=A0</p><=
/blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The basic deal with options is tha=
t when you buy an option from a dealer, the dealer will hedge the option by=
 buying or selling the underlying stock;=C2=A0in particular <a href=3D"http=
s://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/25806429.278654/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZ=
2x5bmhvbHRvbi5jb20vbm90ZXMvZHluYW1pY19oZWRnaW5nLw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB=
0122d062" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" =
style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wi=
dth: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgro=
und-color: #ccc;">the dealer will adjust its hedge</a> by buying the stock =
when it goes up and selling it when it goes down. This makes the stock more=
 volatile: When it goes up, options dealers are buying and pushing it up mo=
re; when it goes down, they=E2=80=99re selling=C2=A0and pushing it down mor=
e. Dealers who sell options are said to be =E2=80=9Cselling volatility.=E2=
=80=9D They produce volatility with their trading and sell it to customers.=
 Customers want a lot of Tesla volatility. So a lot of Tesla volatility is =
produced and delivered to them. The market gives people what they want.</p>=
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The block quote is from a story from Robin Wig=
glesworth in the Financial Times about some of the weird stuff floating aro=
und Tesla:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The real importance =
and wider footprint of what might be called the =E2=80=9CTesla-financial co=
mplex=E2=80=9D far outstrips the company=E2=80=99s market capitalisation. T=
his is thanks to a vast, tangled web of dependent investment vehicles, corp=
orate emulators and an enormous associated derivatives market of unparallel=
ed breadth, depth and hyperactivity.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Combin=
ed, these factors mean Tesla=E2=80=99s influence over the ebb and flow of t=
he stock market is far greater than even its size would imply. It may even =
be historically unrivalled in its wider impact, some analysts say.</p><p st=
yle=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CWe don=E2=80=99t really have the language =

=9D</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And:</p><blockquote><p sty=

igh valuations off the back of social media hype. =E2=80=9CShorting Tesla i=
s just an ego trade at this stage. Tesla has been a primary contributor to =
destroying the credibility of active management over the past few years.=E2=

olor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; =
border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color:=
 #ccc;">Adam Davidson</a> on the dream of Web3=C2=A0for hiring:</p><blockqu=
ote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The conceit of web3 is that an online iden=
tity that can not be faked or altered offers a new level of trust and trans=
parency. (This word, transparency, may not be the right one. On the one han=
d, your blockchain identity is fully transparent. Anyone can see every sing=
le thing ever done with that identity. On the other hand, it is trivially e=
asy to prevent anyone from knowing who, in the real world, that identity be=
longs to.) =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">With web3, a candidate=
 can share a far more robust career story than whatever is shown on their L=
inkedIn profile. They can show not just what titles they had, but what proj=
ects they worked on, what courses they took, what communities they contribu=
ted to.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Of course, someone could tell you a=
ll that now. But resumes are, typically, works of creative aspirational fic=
tion. With web3, people can share tokens showing someone=E2=80=99s crucial =
role on a project. This happens, now, on DAOs (more on those here), blockch=
ain-based communities. I spend a lot of time at the Bankless DAO, where it =
is just a routine part of the daily flow for people to send tokens of grati=
tude to those who have been helpful in some project or other. People who at=
tend meetings, get a POAP, a token of attendance. So, you can see at a glan=
ce if someone is an active member or an occasional lurker. Every day, peopl=
e send =E2=80=9Ctips=E2=80=9D of tokens of gratitude to others. One person =
giving a particularly helpful colleague a tip often leads to an avalanche o=
f others doing the same. (Come to think of it, I really haven=E2=80=99t got=
ten a lot of tips. I should up my game.)</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">As=
 web3 becomes more ubiquitous (as I expect it to), younger employees will a=
sk their bosses and co-workers and, eventually, demand of them that they im=
plement this kind of in-real-time, collaborative credentialing.</p><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Similarly, people can build up a collection of immutab=
le tokens showing the conferences they attended, the courses they took, the=
 skills they proved, the books they read, the creators they supported.</p><=
p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I expect it to become routine--first among youn=
ger people in more technical fields--that recruitment will involve looking =
at someone=E2=80=99s blockchain-backed identity to see a richer picture of =
the person=E2=80=99s background. If a candidate with an unpromising resume =
can demonstrate an unusually large number of these Gold Star tokens--in whi=
ch former bosses and colleagues sent digital expressions of gratitude--from=
 their past, you might overlook the fact that they didn=E2=80=99t, quite, g=
raduate college. Alternatively, if someone with pristine credentials has a =
dearth of such expressions of gratitude, you might look at them more critic=
ally.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And in particular here h=
e is on the dream of Web3 for=C2=A0ad hoc, project-based hiring:</p><blockq=
uote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you want to achieve some quick result-=
-design an app, create a conference, build a new kind of bicycle, whatever-=
-that you couldn=E2=80=99t do on your own, you typically have to spend a to=
n of time and resources NOT doing the thing you care about. You probably ne=
ed to hire people, which means you need to raise money, which means you nee=
d a business plan and have to spend most of your time pitching. =E2=80=A6</=
p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">With web3, you could announce your goal publ=
icly. People who are interested will reach out. You can quickly see what th=
ey=E2=80=99ve done (see above) and choose the team that meets your requirem=
ents. You can create a coin or token and distribute it among the team membe=
rs. You can have a system that grants more of these coins when someone is r=
ecognized by the rest of the team for a helpful contribution. Ownership, th=
en, will be dynamic and will reflect real activity.</p><p style=3D"margin: =
16px 0;">When the thing you all are building launches, the profits can be d=
istributed through the coin system, and the people who added the most value=
 will get the most reward.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Isn=
=E2=80=99t that great? I mean, in general the potential=C2=A0future he desc=
ribes is both intriguing and also horrifying, but here I am just talking ab=
out hit men. If you want to achieve some quick result =E2=80=94=C2=A0murder=
 your husband =E2=80=94=C2=A0you can announce your goal publicly and people=
 who are interested will reach out. You can quickly see what they=E2=80=99v=
e done and choose the hit man who meets your requirements. A hit man who ha=
s received a lot of Good Job Killing My Husband tokens from satisfied custo=
mers will be an attractive candidate, and you might pay a premium for his s=
ervices. A hit man who has received a lot of FBI Gold Star tokens from the =
Federal Bureau of Investigation would be a very=C2=A0<em>bad=C2=A0</em>cand=
idate and you should=C2=A0<em>not=C2=A0</em>hire him despite his no doubt v=
ery reasonable rates. Immutable transparent digital identity! The fact that=
 you have posted your goal publicly probably isn=E2=80=99t great for your o=
dds of not getting arrested, I don=E2=80=99t know.</p><table class=3D"heade=

rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">model of the firm</a> is=
 that firms exist to optimize transaction costs. Some transactions occur in=

ew project, and so in practice companies exist with permanent salaried empl=
oyees who can be told to do new projects without going through new market t=
ransactions. One promise of the internet was that it would reduce transacti=
on costs: Technology makes it easier for buyers and sellers of goods and se=
rvices to coordinate with each other, so they don=E2=80=99t need the coordi=
nating abilities of the firm. In recent years this has led to a surge of in=
terest in non-firm ways of coordinating behavior, from gig-economy platform=
s (lots of people drive for Uber, but are not=C2=A0<em>employees of Uber</e=
m>) to the Web3 projects that Davidson writes about to decentralized autono=
mous organizations in which people pool together to make joint decisions wi=
thout a corporate management structure.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On =

onDAO, failed to buy the Constitution, and now can=E2=80=99t get their mone=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In its "how to donate" video, a member of Cons=
titutionDAO recommended that donors add "recommend adding about $150 to $20=
0 more than you'd like to contribute" to their donations to pay gas fees, w=
hich are transaction fees on the Ethereum network. Motherboard contributed =
a small amount of money to the project to see how this would play out in pr=
actice. Here is how it worked:</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Constitution=
DAO accepted only ether, the token on Ethereum. For someone to convert USD =
to $PEOPLE tokens, the process had several steps. First, we had to buy Ethe=
reum on an exchange (we used Coinbase). We bought $200 worth of Ethereum. C=
oinbase took a $3 fee. Then, we had to send the Ethereum from Coinbase to a=
 MetaMask crypto wallet. To do this, we had to pay a $12 network fee. Then,=
 we had to send the Ethereum from MetaMask to Juicebox. So-called "gas" fee=
s vary wildly and depend on how busy the Ethereum network is at any given m=
oment and the complexity of the transaction. Right now, gas fees on Ethereu=
m are very high, and a highly complex operation could end up costing hundre=
ds of dollars in fees. In our case, we paid a $75 gas fee to contribute rou=
ghly $75 to the project. Of the initial $200 we bought in ETH, $90 was eate=
n up in fees simply to donate to ConstitutionDAO. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"=
margin: 16px 0;">In order to get a refund, we have to do this in reverse, b=
asically. And so to get our ETH =C2=A0back from Juicebox, we would have to =
pay gas fees again, meaning essentially the entirety of the amount invested=
 would be wiped out.=C2=A0=E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Interac=

 $18 for their $0 refund. Here's someone else getting .018 ETH ($76) refund=
ed and paying .0175 ETH ($74) in fees, so they got $2 back when all was sai=
d and done.=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Honestly thi=
